metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an acceptance criterion (and a corresponding unit-test fixture case) for `first_pass_verification`: e.g., 'given 3 tickets where 2 pass on their first COMPLETION_VERDICT, the metric returns 2/3; a ticket with a FAIL then a PASS first-attempt counts as not-first-pass.'
evidence: Metric definitions states: 'first_pass_verification — fraction of tickets whose FIRST COMPLETION_VERDICT record is a completion_verifier_pass_v1 (passed on the first verification attempt).'
Tests section covers: cost_per_accepted_change (2 cases), env_diagnosis_interval (2 cases) — first_pass_verification is not mentioned.
Acceptance Criteria has 4 items; none tests or asserts anything about first_pass_verification.
ABSENCE: no AC maps to the first_pass_verification metric despite it being a named deliverable.
findings.finding: The `first_pass_verification` metric is fully defined in the Metric definitions section but has no acceptance criterion and no test case — no step in the plan verifies it was implemented correctly, leaving the end-state unreachable for this metric.
findings.location: Metric definitions / Acceptance Criteria (gap: first_pass_verification absent from ACs)
scenarios: An implementing agent writes the metric but gets the logic wrong (e.g., counts all PASS records instead of only the FIRST one); no AC catches the defect.
Completion review cannot confirm the metric is implemented because no criterion verifies it.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Acceptance Criteria: '- [ ] `first_pass_verification` over a fixture of 3 tickets — 2 whose first `COMPLETION_VERDICT` is `completion_verifier_pass_v1` and 1 whose first is a failure — returns 2/3 (asserted as a float within epsilon).' Add the matching fixture + assertion to `test_sidecar_metrics.py`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that env_diagnosis_interval pairs each gate_error_v1 ERROR with the next PASS whose `gate` field matches (or whose schema corresponds to the same gate's normal verdict schema), so plan_review and code_review ERRORs are not cross-paired on the shared REVIEW_RESULT stream.
evidence: build_gate_error_payload emits {schema, verdict, gate, error} — the `gate` field distinguishes plan_review from code_review, both of which ride REVIEW_RESULT (gate_error_sidecar.py:44-49, _GATE_EVENT_TYPE maps plan_review and code_review both to REVIEW_RESULT)
Plan says: 'pair each gate_error_v1 ERROR record with the next PASS record on the SAME ticket's stream (raw ordered scan)' — no gate-field filter specified
A ticket that has both a code_review gate outage and a subsequent plan_review PASS (or vice versa) on the same REVIEW_RESULT file-suffix stream would produce a spurious cross-gate interval
findings.finding: The env_diagnosis_interval raw-scan pairing mechanism is under-specified for the case where both plan_review and code_review gates share the REVIEW_RESULT stream on the same ticket: a code_review gate_error_v1 ERROR followed by a plan_review PASS (or vice versa) would produce a spurious cross-gate diagnosis interval, because the plan says 'next PASS record on the SAME ticket's stream' without specifying that the scan must also filter by the `gate` field present in every gate_error_v1 payload.
findings.location: Metric definitions — env_diagnosis_interval; Data-access ground truth section
scenarios: A ticket undergoes a code_review gate outage (code_review gate_error_v1 on REVIEW_RESULT), then receives a plan_review PASS (plan_review_result_v2 on REVIEW_RESULT): the scan pairs the code_review ERROR with the plan_review PASS, yielding a meaningless interval
Any active project where both plan-review and code-review gates run on the same ticket
findings.suggested_fix: In the env_diagnosis_interval definition, add: 'When scanning REVIEW_RESULT files, the ERROR→PASS pairing must respect the `gate` field of the gate_error_v1 record — match only subsequent records from the same gate (e.g., a plan_review ERROR pairs with the next plan_review_result_v1/v2 PASS; a code_review ERROR pairs with the next code_review_result schema PASS), so a plan_review PASS does not close an open code_review diagnosis interval.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconcile the `latest_pass_record` helper claim: either replace it with a raw ordered scan of all COMPLETION_VERDICT files (mirroring the gate_error_v1 scan pattern) or introduce a new `all_pass_records(ticket_id, ...)` helper, and update the Data-access ground truth and Approach sections accordingly.
evidence: Plan: 'Sidecars are reducer-ignored; read them via the raw event files or the ready helpers `rebar.llm.plan_review.sidecar.all_review_results(id, repo_root=)` and `rebar.llm.completion_sidecar.latest_pass_record(id, repo_root=)`'

`latest_pass_record` docstring: 'Return the **most-recent** PASS `COMPLETION_VERDICT` sidecar payload for `ticket_id`, or `None` when none is usable.' (completion_sidecar.py:137-179) — single record only, no range/all support.

cost_per_accepted_change requires counting ALL accepted closes across ALL tickets in a date range; first_pass_verification requires the OLDEST COMPLETION_VERDICT per ticket — neither is achievable with `latest_pass_record` alone.
A raw ordered scan of COMPLETION_VERDICT files across all tickets is needed, mirroring the gate_error_v1 raw scan the plan already specifies for env_diagnosis_interval.
findings.finding: `latest_pass_record` returns only the single most-recent PASS record for a ticket (completion_sidecar.py:137-179), but `cost_per_accepted_change` needs a COUNT of all `completion_verifier_pass_v1` records across all tickets in a time range, and `first_pass_verification` needs the FIRST (oldest) `COMPLETION_VERDICT` per ticket. The plan lists `latest_pass_record(id, repo_root=)` as a 'ready helper' for these derivations, but that API cannot satisfy either requirement — an unverified capability assertion.
findings.location: Data-access ground truth section / Metric definitions (cost_per_accepted_change, first_pass_verification)
scenarios: Implementer calls `latest_pass_record(tid)` per ticket and counts non-None results — this correctly counts tickets with at least one PASS but miscounts tickets with multiple PASS records (which would only count as 1).
Implementer uses `latest_pass_record` for first_pass_verification — this returns the most RECENT pass, not the FIRST verdict event, so the metric logic would be incorrect.
If the plan is read literally and the helper is used as stated, both `cost_per_accepted_change` and `first_pass_verification` would be silently wrong for multi-pass tickets.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the `latest_pass_record` reference for cost/first-pass derivations with an `all_completion_verdicts(ticket_id, repo_root=)` raw scan (analogous to `all_review_results`) that returns all COMPLETION_VERDICT payloads ordered oldest→newest; use schema filtering on the results to count `completion_verifier_pass_v1` records and identify the first verdict schema.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a data-access protocol for `first_pass_verification`: specify that the implementer should walk each ticket's COMPLETION_VERDICT files in oldest-first filename order, take the first file whose payload schema is in `{completion_verifier_pass_v1, completion_verifier_fail_v1}` (skipping gate_error_v1), and check whether its schema is SCHEMA_PASS.
evidence: Plan: 'first_pass_verification — fraction of tickets whose FIRST COMPLETION_VERDICT record is a completion_verifier_pass_v1'
completion_sidecar.py:137-179: latest_pass_record walks newest→oldest (reversed order), not oldest→newest
ABSENCE: no `all_completion_verdicts` or `first_completion_verdict` helper found in codebase search
ABSENCE: no raw-scan protocol specified for this metric (unlike env_diagnosis_interval which at least names 'raw ordered scan')
findings.finding: The `first_pass_verification` metric (defined in Metric definitions) has no named helper, no raw-scan protocol, and no schema-filter rule specified for retrieving the 'first COMPLETION_VERDICT record' per ticket. The two available helpers (`latest_pass_record` returns the most-recent PASS; `latest_fail_verdict` returns the most-recent FAIL) both return the NEWEST record, not the FIRST/OLDEST. No `all_completion_verdicts` helper exists (verified by search). The plan gives no API path for this metric, unlike the other three which name helpers or scan strategies explicitly.
findings.location: Metric definitions / first_pass_verification
scenarios: Implementer uses latest_pass_record and gets the most-recent PASS, not the first verdict — metric is computed incorrectly for any ticket with multiple close attempts
Implementer must invent a raw scan reading files in oldest-first order with a schema guard, with no guidance from the plan

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an Acceptance Criterion for `first_pass_verification` — e.g. assert that a fixture with 3 tickets where 2 have their first COMPLETION_VERDICT as a pass record yields 2/3, and a fixture with 0 tickets returns `None`.
evidence: Metric Definitions defines: 'first_pass_verification — fraction of tickets whose FIRST COMPLETION_VERDICT record is a completion_verifier_pass_v1 (passed on the first verification attempt).'
No AC references first_pass_verification, its computation, or any fixture that asserts its value.
findings.finding: The `first_pass_verification` metric is described in the Metric Definitions section but has no covering Acceptance Criterion; it is an uncovered deliverable.
findings.location: Metric definitions / Acceptance Criteria
scenarios: An implementer could ship first_pass_verification with a wrong denominator (e.g. counting all tickets rather than only those with at least one COMPLETION_VERDICT) and no test would catch it.
A reviewer has no way to declare the metric done without an acceptance criterion.
findings.suggested_fix: Add AC: 'first_pass_verification over a fixture of 3 tickets where 2 have their first COMPLETION_VERDICT as completion_verifier_pass_v1 returns 2/3; a fixture with 0 tickets returns None — both asserted.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a test fixture and AC for `first_pass_verification`: at minimum a happy-path case (e.g., 2 tickets, one passes on first attempt, one does not → fraction = 0.5) and a zero-tickets edge case (returns `None`/`Unavailable`, no ZeroDivisionError).
evidence: Metric definitions: 'first_pass_verification — fraction of tickets whose FIRST COMPLETION_VERDICT record is a completion_verifier_pass_v1'
Tests section lists only: cost_per_accepted_change fixtures, ERROR+PASS interval, ERROR-no-PASS open interval
ACs 1–4 cover: env_diagnosis_interval, cost_per_accepted_change, llm_calls source guard, registry registration — first_pass_verification appears in none
findings.finding: The `first_pass_verification` metric is defined in Metric Definitions but has no corresponding test fixture, assertion, or Acceptance Criterion anywhere in the plan — it is a new user-facing metric with zero stated test coverage.
findings.location: Metric definitions / Tests section / Acceptance Criteria
scenarios: The metric is implemented but untested; a bug in the 'first record only' or schema-filter logic is undetectable at ship time
The denominator (tickets with at least one COMPLETION_VERDICT) could be zero, which is an unguarded edge analogous to the zero-division case explicitly covered for cost_per_accepted_change
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Tests: 'first_pass_verification over 2 tickets where ticket A's first COMPLETION_VERDICT is completion_verifier_pass_v1 and ticket B's first is a fail record returns 0.5; over 0 tickets returns None.' Add a corresponding AC5.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (or extend AC#3) that asserts summed latency fields — e.g. a fixture with two REVIEW_RESULT records carrying `det_ms=100,llm_ms=200,total_ms=300` and `det_ms=50,llm_ms=150,total_ms=200` yields aggregated latency values of `det_ms=150, llm_ms=350, total_ms=500`.
evidence: Metric Definitions: 'sum REVIEW_RESULT.metrics.llm_calls (and latency fields) over the range. source="sidecar".'
AC#3 tests that llm_calls is read from REVIEW_RESULT (not COMPLETION_VERDICT) but does not assert correct summation of det_ms, llm_ms, or total_ms.
No AC asserts a fixture with known latency values yields the correct summed latency output.
findings.finding: The `llm_call_proxy/latency` metric description includes aggregation of latency fields (`det_ms`, `llm_ms`, `total_ms`) over the range, but no Acceptance Criterion verifies the latency aggregation — only the `llm_calls` source constraint is tested (AC#3 via the cost derivation path).
findings.location: Metric definitions ('llm_call_proxy / latency') / Acceptance Criteria
scenarios: Latency fields could be silently dropped, mis-keyed, or not exposed in the metric output and no test would fail.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the API or raw-scan protocol for enumerating all `completion_verifier_pass_v1` records across all tickets in a time range (e.g. walk all ticket dirs, collect all COMPLETION_VERDICT files, filter by schema and timestamp) — `latest_pass_record` returns only the single most-recent PASS for one ticket and cannot perform this aggregation.
evidence: Plan: 'Sidecars are reducer-ignored; read them via … rebar.llm.completion_sidecar.latest_pass_record(id, repo_root=)'
completion_sidecar.py:137-179: latest_pass_record returns 'the **most-recent** PASS … for ticket_id' — one record for one ticket, not a count across a range
Plan: 'count of completion_verifier_pass_v1 records (accepted closes) in range' — requires multi-ticket enumeration with no named helper that does this
findings.finding: The `cost_per_accepted_change` computation requires a COUNT of all `completion_verifier_pass_v1` records across all tickets in the time range, but the only named helper `latest_pass_record` (completion_sidecar.py:137-179) returns only the single most-recent PASS record for a single ticket — it cannot enumerate or count across tickets or time ranges. No alternative API, raw-scan protocol, or iteration strategy is specified for this aggregation.
findings.location: Metric definitions / cost_per_accepted_change; Data-access ground truth paragraph 2
scenarios: Implementer calls latest_pass_record per ticket but has no way to enumerate all tickets in range or count multiple PASSes per ticket — cost_per_accepted_change is computed incorrectly (always 0 or 1 per ticket)
A ticket with two successful closes (reopened and reclosed) counts as 1 accepted change even if the intent is 2
findings.suggested_fix: Add to 'Data-access ground truth': 'To count accepted closes in range: walk each ticket dir in the tracker, list all `*-COMPLETION_VERDICT.json` files, filter by timestamp within range, load and filter by `schema == SCHEMA_PASS`; `latest_pass_record` is not used here.' Alternatively name an `all_pass_records(ticket_id)` helper analogous to `all_review_results`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in the Approach / Metric definitions whether `env_diagnosis_interval` scans REVIEW_RESULT files, COMPLETION_VERDICT files, or both per ticket, and specify how ERROR records from different gate streams are paired with their subsequent PASS on the correct stream.
evidence: gate_error_sidecar.py:31-35: `_GATE_EVENT_TYPE = {'plan_review': 'REVIEW_RESULT', 'code_review': 'REVIEW_RESULT', 'completion': 'COMPLETION_VERDICT'}`
A ticket that hits both a plan-review ERROR and a completion ERROR will have ERROR records on two different file streams
The plan says 'same ticket's stream' (singular) but does not specify which stream to scan when both could carry gate_error_v1 records
findings.finding: The `env_diagnosis_interval` raw ordered scan is underspecified regarding which host event stream(s) to scan per ticket: `gate_error_v1` records ride on REVIEW_RESULT for plan/code-review gates AND on COMPLETION_VERDICT for the completion gate (as defined in gate_error_sidecar.py:31-35). The plan says 'raw ordered scan of those event files' without specifying whether the implementer must scan both streams or only one.
findings.location: Data-access ground truth: 'gate_error_v1 (ERROR) rides the host stream (REVIEW_RESULT/COMPLETION_VERDICT) ... ERROR/PASS pairing uses a raw ordered scan of those event files'
scenarios: If only REVIEW_RESULT files are scanned, completion-gate ERROR→PASS intervals are missed.
If only COMPLETION_VERDICT files are scanned, plan/code-review ERROR intervals are missed.
An implementation that scans both streams independently might double-count or produce incorrect intervals if not unified.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the metric definition: 'For each ticket, scan both REVIEW_RESULT and COMPLETION_VERDICT event files; pair each gate_error_v1 ERROR with the next non-ERROR, non-gate_error_v1 record on the SAME file stream (same event type) for that ticket.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify whether gate_error_v1 records on COMPLETION_VERDICT are excluded when determining the 'first COMPLETION_VERDICT record' for first_pass_verification (e.g., 'first non-ERROR COMPLETION_VERDICT record'), so infra outages are not counted as failed first-pass attempts.
evidence: gate_error_sidecar.py _GATE_EVENT_TYPE maps 'completion' to 'COMPLETION_VERDICT' — so gate_error_v1 records from completion gate outages ride COMPLETION_VERDICT, the same stream scanned for first_pass_verification
Plan says: 'fraction of tickets whose FIRST COMPLETION_VERDICT record is a completion_verifier_pass_v1' — no exclusion of gate_error_v1 records from the 'first record' determination
build_gate_error_payload produces schema=gate_error_v1, verdict=ERROR — structurally distinct from both SCHEMA_PASS and SCHEMA, but the plan's definition treats it as a 'not PASS' outcome
findings.finding: The first_pass_verification metric definition does not address the edge case where a ticket's first COMPLETION_VERDICT event is a gate_error_v1 (infra outage at the completion gate), which rides COMPLETION_VERDICT but is not a verification attempt — counting it as 'did not pass on first attempt' is incorrect and silently skews the metric downward during infrastructure instability.
findings.location: Metric definitions — first_pass_verification
scenarios: Infra outage occurs during a ticket's first-ever completion gate run: a gate_error_v1 record is emitted to COMPLETION_VERDICT; the next attempt succeeds (completion_verifier_pass_v1); the metric counts this ticket as 'not first-pass verified' even though the first real verification attempt succeeded

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify the REVIEW_RESULT.metrics block enumeration in 'Data-access ground truth': the actual dict (gate_dispatch.py:281-290) has six keys — add `verify_requests` to the plan's field list so sidecar_metrics.py and its fixtures cover the complete shape.
evidence: Plan states: 'REVIEW_RESULT.metrics` — `{det_ms, llm_ms, total_ms, llm_calls, claim_path}` (`plan_review/sidecar.py:496-497`)'
gate_dispatch.py:281-290 emits: det_ms, llm_ms, total_ms, llm_calls, verify_requests, claim_path (six keys)
sidecar.py:496 comment repeats only the 5-field list, omitting verify_requests — comment and plan are in sync but both diverge from the actual dict
findings.finding: The plan's 'data-access ground truth' section enumerates the REVIEW_RESULT.metrics block as exactly {det_ms, llm_ms, total_ms, llm_calls, claim_path} (citing sidecar.py:496-497), but the actual dict constructed in gate_dispatch.py:281-290 emits a sixth field — verify_requests — not mentioned in the plan. The code comment at sidecar.py:496 echoes only the five fields, so an implementer reading this plan will derive the metrics block shape from the plan's enumeration and may silently omit verify_requests from any passthrough or schema validation in the new sidecar_metrics.py module.
findings.location: Data-access ground truth section — 'plan_review/sidecar.py:496-497' citation; also gate_dispatch.py:281-290
scenarios: If sidecar_metrics.py validates or selectively reads the metrics sub-dict using the plan's 5-field enumeration, verify_requests will be silently dropped from any forwarded/aggregated structure.
Test fixtures built from the plan's enumeration will not include verify_requests, so any schema-level assertion on the metrics block will be incomplete.
findings.suggested_fix: Update the plan's ground-truth enumeration to: `{det_ms, llm_ms, total_ms, llm_calls, verify_requests, claim_path}` and ensure the sidecar_metrics.py implementation and test fixtures reflect all six fields.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add the ticket-enumeration mechanism (e.g., list all ticket dirs in tracker) to the Approach section so the cross-ticket range scan for `llm_call_proxy` is fully specified.
evidence: all_review_results signature at plan_review/sidecar.py:164: `def all_review_results(ticket_id: str, *, repo_root=None) -> list[dict[str, Any]]`
llm_call_proxy metric defined as 'sum REVIEW_RESULT.metrics.llm_calls ... over the range' — implies multi-ticket iteration
No ticket-enumeration helper is named anywhere in the plan
findings.finding: The plan names `all_review_results(id, repo_root=)` as a 'ready helper' for the range-based `llm_call_proxy` metric, but this helper is per-ticket (takes a single `ticket_id`). The plan provides no mechanism or named helper to enumerate tickets within a date/range, leaving the cross-ticket iteration strategy unspecified for the implementer.
findings.location: Approach section / Data-access ground truth: 'ready helpers rebar.llm.plan_review.sidecar.all_review_results(id, repo_root=)'
scenarios: Implementer must discover independently how to list all tickets (e.g., scan tracker dir) before calling all_review_results per ticket — this pattern is unenumerated and could be implemented inconsistently.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add to the Approach section the cross-ticket enumeration strategy for range-scoped aggregation: specify whether the reader enumerates tickets via list_tickets(), a raw tracker directory walk, or another mechanism, and how timestamp range filtering is applied at the ticket-collection level (not just at the per-sidecar payload level).
evidence: all_review_results(ticket_id, repo_root=) is per-ticket — plan cites it as the read helper but says 'sum REVIEW_RESULT.metrics.llm_calls over the range' without specifying how tickets are enumerated for that range
latest_pass_record(ticket_id) is also per-ticket — 'count of completion_verifier_pass_v1 records in range' likewise requires a cross-ticket enumeration first
ABSENCE: no mention of list_tickets(), tracker directory walk, or any other enumeration mechanism in the plan
findings.finding: The range-scoped aggregation mechanism (llm_call_proxy, cost_per_accepted_change) has an unspecified cross-ticket enumeration step: the existing helpers (all_review_results, latest_pass_record) are per-ticket, but summing llm_calls or counting completion_verifier_pass_v1 records 'over the range' requires first enumerating which tickets to include. The plan never specifies this enumeration strategy (e.g., via list_tickets(), or scanning the tracker directory directly), leaving a data-flow gap in the aggregation mechanism.
findings.location: Metric definitions — llm_call_proxy / latency; cost_per_accepted_change; Approach section
scenarios: Implementer must invent the enumeration strategy (list_tickets? walk tracker dir? filter by timestamp?), making inconsistent choices that affect which records fall 'in range'
Without a specified enumeration order and timestamp filtering strategy, the 'range' parameter has no defined meaning at the ticket-collection level

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the concrete access mechanism for bulk/chronological COMPLETION_VERDICT reads needed by `first_pass_verification` (chronologically-first verdict per ticket) and `cost_per_accepted_change` (all PASS records in a date range across all tickets) — either name a new `all_completion_verdicts(ticket_id, repo_root=)` helper or explicitly extend the 'raw ordered scan' approach to cover these metrics and describe how all tickets are enumerated.
evidence: Plan: 'ready helpers `rebar.llm.plan_review.sidecar.all_review_results(id, repo_root=)` and `rebar.llm.completion_sidecar.latest_pass_record(id, repo_root=)`' — but latest_pass_record returns only the most-recent PASS, not all PASS records
completion_sidecar.py has no `all_completion_verdicts()` or equivalent bulk reader (confirmed by full-file search for 'def all')
Plan: 'ERROR/PASS pairing uses a raw ordered scan of those event files' — this raw-scan mechanism is only explicitly named for gate_error_v1, not for first_pass_verification or cost counting
first_pass_verification definition: 'fraction of tickets whose FIRST COMPLETION_VERDICT record is a completion_verifier_pass_v1' — requires chronological-first record, not most-recent

cost_per_accepted_change: 'count of completion_verifier_pass_v1 records (accepted closes) in range' — requires counting all PASS records across all tickets in a date range; no range-aware helper exists
findings.finding: The `first_pass_verification` and `cost_per_accepted_change` metrics both require bulk/chronological access to ALL `COMPLETION_VERDICT` records across a date range or per-ticket history, but no such reader exists in `completion_sidecar.py`. `latest_pass_record` returns only the most-recent PASS; `all_review_results` has no COMPLETION_VERDICT counterpart. The plan names 'raw ordered scan' for gate_error_v1 only — it does not specify this mechanism for the COMPLETION_VERDICT stream used by these two metrics, leaving the implementation path for enumerating all tickets' COMPLETION_VERDICT files in a time range unspecified.
findings.location: Metric definitions — `first_pass_verification` and `cost_per_accepted_change`; Data-access ground truth section
scenarios: Implementer reaches for `latest_pass_record` for first_pass_verification but gets the most-recent PASS, not the chronologically first one — silently produces wrong results for any ticket with multiple PASS verdicts.

Implementer has no named helper for 'enumerate all tickets in the tracker and count their COMPLETION_VERDICT PASS records in a date range' — must write bespoke directory-walking code not described in the plan.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `all_completion_verdicts(ticket_id, repo_root=)` helper to `completion_sidecar.py` mirroring `all_review_results` (returning all COMPLETION_VERDICT payloads newest→oldest). For `cost_per_accepted_change` and `first_pass_verification`, add a sentence in the plan explaining how all tickets are enumerated (e.g. `os.listdir(tracker_dir)`) and filtered by date range using the filename ns-prefix timestamps, consistent with how gate_error_v1 raw-scan is described.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the exact filter rule for the 'next PASS' in the `env_diagnosis_interval` raw scan: specify which event file suffix to scan (same stream as the ERROR?), which schema(s) and/or `verdict` value qualify as a PASS, and whether the `gate` field on the ERROR record scopes the search.
evidence: Plan: 'pair each gate_error_v1 ERROR record with the next PASS record on the SAME ticket's stream (raw ordered scan)'
gate_error_sidecar.py:31-35: gate_error_v1 records ride REVIEW_RESULT (plan_review/code_review) OR COMPLETION_VERDICT (completion) depending on gate
Plan never specifies: filter by same event type? filter by gate field? what schema does a 'PASS record' have in the raw scan context?
ABSENCE: no schema/verdict filter expression given for what qualifies as a 'next PASS' in the raw scan
findings.finding: The `env_diagnosis_interval` ERROR→PASS pairing rule for the raw ordered scan never defines what constitutes a qualifying 'PASS record' in the raw stream. Both REVIEW_RESULT and COMPLETION_VERDICT streams carry `gate_error_v1` records (verdict=ERROR) AND normal verdict records (verdict=PASS). The plan does not say whether: (a) only same-stream events qualify as the next PASS, (b) the `gate` field on the ERROR record is used to scope the search, or (c) any downstream PASS on either stream counts. Additionally, schema-guarded readers skip gate_error_v1 records, so the raw scan must use a different filter — but no filter expression is given.
findings.location: Metric definitions / env_diagnosis_interval; Data-access ground truth paragraph 3
scenarios: A code_review gate_error_v1 rides REVIEW_RESULT; the next REVIEW_RESULT event is a plan_review PASS — does this pair? The plan is silent.
A completion gate_error_v1 rides COMPLETION_VERDICT; the next raw event is a completion_verifier_fail_v1 with verdict=FAIL — the raw scan must skip this, but no filter is specified.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the env_diagnosis_interval definition: 'Raw scan reads files matching the same event-type suffix as the ERROR record (REVIEW_RESULT or COMPLETION_VERDICT, inferred from the gate field); a PASS record is any event whose payload.verdict == "PASS" AND payload.schema is in the non-error sidecar schemas for that stream (plan_review_result_v1/v2 for REVIEW_RESULT; completion_verifier_pass_v1 for COMPLETION_VERDICT).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the concrete return value for an open `env_diagnosis_interval` (ERROR with no following PASS) and add a matching `assert` expression to the Tests section (e.g. `assert interval is None` or `assert interval['closed'] is False`).
evidence: Plan: 'An ERROR with no following PASS is an open (unclosed) diagnosis interval'
Tests section: 'an ERROR with no PASS yields an open interval' — no assertion form given (no `assert` expression, no return type specified)
ABSENCE: no specification of whether open intervals are returned as None, excluded from aggregation, returned as a sentinel (e.g. {'open': True, 'start': ts}), or raise
findings.finding: The return-value representation for an open (unclosed) `env_diagnosis_interval` — 'An ERROR with no following PASS' — is never specified. The plan mentions the concept but gives no concrete definition of what the metric returns or how the test asserts it, beyond saying it 'yields an open interval'.
findings.location: Metric definitions / env_diagnosis_interval; Tests section
scenarios: Implementer returns None for open intervals; test asserts a list length and silently passes/fails incorrectly
Implementer excludes open intervals from the metric; a persistent infra outage is invisible in the output

findings.finding: The plan does not specify what the implementation should return or do when an ERROR sidecar has no subsequent PASS on the same ticket — the executor must guess (skip/None/raise/sentinel).
findings.location: Approach section — 'env-diagnosis pairs each ERROR with the next PASS on the same ticket'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Approach: 'If an ERROR has no subsequent PASS on the same ticket, the interval is excluded from results (the entry is skipped).'

findings.finding: The plan does not specify the fallback behavior when an ERROR sidecar record has no subsequent PASS on the same ticket. The Approach says 'env-diagnosis pairs each ERROR with the next PASS' but is silent on what the implementation should do for an unresolved ERROR (the most recent event, or a ticket that closed in FAIL). An implementer must choose between: skip/exclude the entry, return None/sentinel, raise ValueError, or leave the interval open-ended.
findings.location: Approach section: 'env-diagnosis pairs each ERROR with the next PASS on the same ticket'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Approach: 'An ERROR with no subsequent PASS on the same ticket is excluded from the diagnosis-interval list (open intervals are not emitted).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the concrete return representation for an open (unclosed) `env_diagnosis_interval` — e.g., 'returns `None` for the duration field' or 'returns a dict `{start: ts, end: None, duration_ms: None}`' — so the test and implementation agree without executor guesswork.
evidence: Metric definitions: 'An ERROR with no following PASS is an open (unclosed) diagnosis interval.'
AC1: 'an ERROR with no PASS yields an open interval' — no type, no sentinel, no None/special-object specification.
An executor must choose between None, a dict with a None 'end' key, a special OpenInterval object, or raising an exception — all reasonable but mutually incompatible.
findings.finding: The return value for an 'open' (unclosed) `env_diagnosis_interval` — an ERROR with no following PASS — is not specified anywhere in the plan; the AC only says it 'yields an open interval' without stating the concrete type or sentinel value the implementation must return.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC1 / Metric definitions — env_diagnosis_interval
scenarios: Two developers independently implement the function and return different types for the open-interval case; downstream consumers break on one variant.
The test fixture for the open-interval case cannot be written until the return type is decided, potentially blocking test authorship.
findings.suggested_fix: In the Metric definitions section, add: 'An open interval is represented as `{"start": <error_ts>, "end": None, "duration_ms": None}` (or equivalently, the reader yields the record with `duration=None`). The AC fixture asserts `dur is None`.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Capture the raw-ordered-file-scan pattern (and when to use it vs. schema-guarded helpers) in AGENTS.md or a brief ADR so future metrics authors have explicit guidance.
evidence: 'gate_error_v1 (ERROR) rides the host stream (REVIEW_RESULT/COMPLETION_VERDICT) and is invisible to the schema-guarded verdict readers, so ERROR/PASS pairing uses a raw ordered scan of those event files'
No ADR, AGENTS.md update, or design-doc section is mentioned anywhere in the plan.
The plan adds sidecar_metrics.py as a new reader pattern that future metrics authors may need to follow or deliberately avoid.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a novel pattern — bypassing schema-guarded readers via raw ordered file scan for ERROR/PASS pairing — that is explained inline but not captured in an ADR, AGENTS.md update, or design-doc entry. Other metrics authors will not know when to apply this escape hatch versus the schema-guarded helpers.
findings.location: Approach section / 'Data-access ground truth' section (gate_error_v1 raw scan description)
scenarios: A future metrics author needs to read an event type that doesn't surface through schema-guarded helpers and doesn't know the raw-scan pattern is the approved escape hatch.
A refactor of the event file naming convention (ns-prefix) silently breaks the raw scan with no documented decision record to consult.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a short note to AGENTS.md (or a `docs/adr/NNN-raw-event-scan.md`) stating: 'For event types invisible to schema-guarded readers (e.g. gate_error_v1 ERROR), use a raw ordered scan of event files keyed by the ns-prefix timestamp. Prefer schema-guarded helpers for all other access.' Reference this from the module docstring of sidecar_metrics.py.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify error-handling behavior for file I/O and helper failures in `sidecar_metrics.py`: define whether missing/corrupt sidecar files are skipped-with-log, raise a typed exception, or return a partial result, AND add at least one observability point (log warning or increment an error counter) so operators can detect and diagnose read failures.
evidence: 'raw ordered scan of those event files' — no error handling mentioned for missing or malformed files
'rebar.llm.plan_review.sidecar.all_review_results(id, repo_root=)' and 'rebar.llm.completion_sidecar.latest_pass_record(id, repo_root=)' — no mention of what happens when these raise
ABSENCE: no logging, alerting, or metric instrumentation for file-read failures is specified anywhere in the plan
ABSENCE: no specification of behavior when a sidecar file is absent (e.g., a ticket that never reached a REVIEW_RESULT event)
findings.finding: The plan specifies raw file I/O (ordered scan of event files) and helper calls (all_review_results, latest_pass_record) but provides no error-handling strategy for I/O failures — missing sidecar files, partially written records, corrupt/unparseable data, or helper exceptions. No logging, metric, or alert is specified for these failure modes, so errors would either propagate unchecked (crashing the metrics computation) or be silently swallowed (returning wrong metric values), with no operator visibility.
findings.location: ## Data-access ground truth / ## Approach / src/rebar/metrics/sidecar_metrics.py
scenarios: A ticket's sidecar file was never written (gate skipped or crashed mid-run) — raw scan raises FileNotFoundError with no catch, aborting the entire metrics computation for the range.
A partially written sidecar record (e.g., gate crashed mid-write) causes a JSON/parse error that is silently swallowed, producing an under-counted llm_calls total with no diagnostic signal.
Helper raises an unexpected exception; without observability the metrics registry silently returns stale or zero values and operators have no way to detect it.
findings.suggested_fix: In the `## Approach` or `## Files` section, add: 'Missing or unparseable sidecar records are skipped and logged at WARNING level with the ticket id and error; a `sidecar_read_error_total` counter is incremented. The metric computation returns a partial result (best-effort over successfully read records) rather than raising, so an isolated bad file does not abort the entire range query. A unit test fixture with a missing/corrupt sidecar file asserts the partial-result behaviour.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate `tests/unit/metrics/test_registry_heldout.py` as a potentially affected consumer of `registry.py`'s `REGISTRY` and verify whether it needs updating when new sidecar `MetricSpec` entries are added.
evidence: tests/unit/metrics/test_registry_heldout.py exists and references SOURCES/AUTHORITATIVE sets
REGISTRY list in registry.py currently has 3 MetricSpec entries; adding new sidecar metrics changes this list
Plan's Files section does not enumerate test_registry_heldout.py as a potentially affected file
findings.finding: `tests/unit/metrics/test_registry_heldout.py` is an existing test that validates `REGISTRY` invariants (structure, unique ids, closed source vocabulary). Adding new `MetricSpec` entries to `REGISTRY` in `registry.py` is an unenumerated consumer/caller impact — the heldout test may pass or fail depending on what it asserts, and the plan does not mention it.
findings.location: Files section: 'registered into src/rebar/metrics/registry.py'
scenarios: If test_registry_heldout.py asserts the exact set of metric ids or the length of REGISTRY, adding new entries without updating it will cause CI failures.

findings.finding: 'What' lists 'finding decisions / false-positive proxy' and 'first-pass verification' as deliverables, but neither the Approach, Tests, nor Acceptance Criteria sections contain any description or coverage of these metrics — they are orphaned deliverables with no implementation plan and no verifying criterion.
findings.location: ## What (lines 3-4) vs ## Approach, ## Tests, ## Acceptance Criteria
findings.suggested_fix: Option A (scope-match): Remove 'finding decisions / false-positive proxy, first-pass verification' from the What section so the plan scope matches its ACs. Option B (full coverage): For each metric, add a sentence to Approach describing the derivation formula, a test case in Tests, and a corresponding AC with an exact expected value.

findings.finding: Three metrics named in 'What' as deliverables — finding decisions / false-positive proxy, first-pass verification, and LLM calls/latency — have no corresponding Acceptance Criterion and no end-state-observable proof of delivery. They are not marked deferred, so they are non-deferred goals without verifiable completion.
findings.location: ## What / ## Acceptance Criteria
findings.suggested_fix: Either add one AC per missing metric (e.g., 'a fixture with two PASS and one REJECT verdict returns a false-positive proxy of 0.33') or move them to an explicit 'Out of scope / future' section so implementers know they are intentionally deferred.

findings.finding: The plan asserts 'The sidecars already persist per-run metrics{llm_calls,det_ms,llm_ms,total_ms}' for both REVIEW_RESULT and COMPLETION_VERDICT sidecars, but the COMPLETION_VERDICT sidecar's build_payload() in completion_sidecar.py stores no 'metrics' key in either the PASS (SCHEMA_PASS) or FAIL (SCHEMA) payload — only findings/criteria/remediation/certifiable/runner/model/material_fingerprint. The llm_calls cost-proxy is absent from COMPLETION_VERDICT records.
findings.location: Approach section: 'The sidecars already persist per-run metrics{llm_calls,det_ms,llm_ms,total_ms}'; src/rebar/llm/completion_sidecar.py build_payload()
findings.suggested_fix: Scope the plan's cost-proxy metrics (llm_calls, det_ms, llm_ms, total_ms) explicitly to REVIEW_RESULT sidecars only, since COMPLETION_VERDICT build_payload() does not lift coverage['metrics']. If completion-gate cost coverage is needed, add a prerequisite to amend completion_sidecar.py:build_payload() to include the metrics field.

findings.finding: The plan asserts cost-per-accepted-change uses `llm_calls` summed across attempts, but `COMPLETION_VERDICT` PASS records (schema `completion_verifier_pass_v1`) carry no `metrics` block at all — only `schema/verdict/ticket_id/criteria/findings/runner/model/material_fingerprint`. If 'accepted (PASS) close' means a completion PASS, the llm_calls signal needed for the numerator comes only from the plan-review `REVIEW_RESULT` sidecar, not the completion sidecar — an implicit assumption the plan never states.
findings.location: Approach section ('cost-per-accepted-change divides summed llm_calls by accepted (PASS) closes') / src/rebar/llm/completion_sidecar.py:build_payload()

findings.finding: The plan states 'The sidecars already persist per-run metrics{llm_calls,det_ms,llm_ms,total_ms}' as a factual premise for cost-per-accepted-change, but inspection of completion_sidecar.py build_payload() confirms that COMPLETION_VERDICT payloads carry NO 'metrics' key in either the PASS branch (schema=completion_verifier_pass_v1) or the FAIL branch (schema=completion_verifier_fail_v1). Only REVIEW_RESULT sidecars embed coverage['metrics'] (confirmed in plan_review/sidecar.py line 497 and code_review/finalize.py line 186). The llm_calls cost-proxy field is absent from all COMPLETION_VERDICT records.
findings.location: Approach section: 'The sidecars already persist per-run metrics{llm_calls,det_ms,llm_ms,total_ms}'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Approach: 'llm_calls is sourced only from REVIEW_RESULT sidecars (plan-review + code-review); COMPLETION_VERDICT sidecars carry no metrics field. cost-per-accepted-change is therefore computed over REVIEW_RESULT PASSes only.'

findings.finding: The plan conflates 'accepted (PASS) close' with a plan-review REVIEW_RESULT PASS verdict. In the codebase, a plan-review PASS is a gate pass — the ticket may still be in-flight. An 'accepted change' (closed ticket) maps to a COMPLETION_VERDICT PASS (emitted at transition_close). Summing llm_calls over REVIEW_RESULT PASSes and calling the denominator 'accepted closes' measures the wrong thing: it counts gate passes, not completed work items, so the metric will be systematically inflated by tickets that pass review but are never closed.
findings.location: Approach section — 'cost-per-accepted-change divides summed llm_calls by accepted (PASS) closes'
findings.suggested_fix: Define the denominator as COMPLETION_VERDICT PASS records (schema='completion_verifier_pass_v1'), which are emitted at transition_close and represent actual accepted closes. Sum llm_calls from all REVIEW_RESULT sidecar events for the same ticket (across all gate passes in the remediation loop) and divide by 1 for each COMPLETION_VERDICT PASS.

findings.finding: The test plan covers only happy-path metric computations but omits two concrete failure paths: (1) cost-per-accepted-change when there are zero PASS (accepted) closes — dividing summed llm_calls by 0 raises ZeroDivisionError or produces an undefined result; (2) env-diagnosis pairing when an ERROR row has no subsequent PASS on the same ticket — the pairing logic's behavior is unspecified and untested.
findings.location: Tests section and Acceptance Criteria — both ACs
findings.suggested_fix: Add two fixture-based unit tests: `test_cost_per_accepted_change_no_accepted` asserting the function returns `None` (or a sentinel) rather than raising when the denominator is zero; and `test_env_diagnosis_unmatched_error` asserting that an ERROR with no following PASS is excluded from (or reported as open-ended in) the interval results. Document the chosen contract in the function docstring.

findings.finding: The Files section lists 'registered in src/rebar/metrics/registry.py' as a deliverable, but no AC verifies the registration was performed — the registry step can be silently omitted and both ACs still pass.
findings.location: Files section — 'src/rebar/metrics/sidecar_metrics.py — new: reader over the sidecars, registered in src/rebar/metrics/registry.py'
findings.suggested_fix: Add AC: '`python -c "from rebar.metrics.registry import get_metrics; names = [m.name for m in get_metrics()]; assert \'cost_per_accepted_change\' in names and \'env_diagnosis_interval\' in names"` exits 0.'

findings.finding: The plan does not specify behavior when there are zero PASS (accepted) closes, making the cost-per-accepted-change formula division-by-zero undefined — the executor must choose a default.
findings.location: Approach section — 'cost-per-accepted-change divides summed llm_calls by accepted (PASS) closes'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Approach: 'When there are no accepted (PASS) closes, cost-per-accepted-change returns None.'

findings.finding: The plan's 'accepted (PASS) closes' term never defines which field and value identify a close as 'accepted': COMPLETION_VERDICT uses schema-tagged routing (completion_verifier_pass_v1 for PASS, completion_verifier_fail_v1 for FAIL), while REVIEW_RESULT uses a plain verdict string (PASS/BLOCK/INDETERMINATE). An implementer reading multiple sidecar streams cannot determine whether to test verdict=='PASS', schema=='completion_verifier_pass_v1', or both — these are structurally distinct membership tests that produce different result sets.
findings.location: Approach section: 'cost-per-accepted-change divides summed llm_calls by accepted (PASS) closes'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
